LAW & MOTION/DISCOVERY, DEPT. 301, AS TO MAY-23-2025 HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF FELICITA FELIX SARAVIA V. DE MONTOYA'S MOTION TO VACATE ORDER COMPELLING JAMS ARBITRATION AND REQUEST FOR SANCTIONS UNDER CAL. CIV. PROC. 1281.99 IN THE AMOUNT OF 13,725; THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. PLAINTIFF FELICITA SARAVIA'S MOTION TO VACATE ORDER COMPELLING ARBITRATION IS GRANTED AND HER REQUEST FOR MONETARY SANCTIONS IS GRANTED IN PART (SEE MINI MINUTES FOR COMPLETE RULING). ORDER SUBMITTED. JUDGE: HAROLD E. KAHN; CLERK: V. DA FONSECA; COURT REPORTER: JAIME PLY, CSR# 13323 (TEL: 714-316-3757 / EMAIL: JPLYCSR@AOL.COM) =(301/HEK)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Plaintiffs Motion To Vacate Order Compelling Arbitration And Award For Sanctions Or, In The Alternative, Invitation To Reconsider
Off Calendar Oct-09-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC.'s Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Plaintiff's Motion To Vacate Order Compelling Arbitration And Award For Sanctions Or, In The Alternative, Invitation To Reconsider.

Defendant Uber Technologies, Inc.'s motion for reconsideration of the order granting plaintiff's motion to vacate the order compelling arbitration and award for sanctions or, in the alternative, invitation to reconsider is granted. The portion of the May 27, 2025 order vacating the May 10, 2023 order granting Uber's motion to compel arbitration is vacated and the May 10, 2023 order granting Uber's motion to compel arbitration is reinstated. The portion of the motion seeking a refund of the sanctions awarded in the May 27, 2025 order is denied.

The court accepts the invitation to reconsider the May 27, 2025 order per Le Francois v. Goel (2005) 35 Cal. 4th 1094 based on the significant change in the law effected by Hohenshelt v. Superior Court regarding waiver of the contractual right to arbitration for untimely payment of arbitration expenses. Plaintiff Felicita Saravia has not filed any opposition to this motion.

Upon reconsideration of the May 27, 2025 order, Hohenshelt compels the vacation of the portion of that order vacating the May 10, 2023 order because there are no facts showing that Uber's failure to timely pay arbitration expenses was willful, fraudulent or grossly negligent. However, the portion of the May 27, 2025 order requiring Uber to pay $6,900 "as a result of the material breach" by Uber is fully consistent with Hohenshelt and thus is not disturbed. The award of $6,900, an amount expressly found to compensate Ms. Saravia, satisfies the "compensation requirement" of Civil Code 3275 as construed in Hohenshelt.

Proposed order submitted.

Visiting Judge: Harold E. Kahn; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/HEK)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Sep-12-2025 Continued To Nov-19-25 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Vacate Order Compelling Jams Arbitration And Request For Sanctions Under Cal. Civ. Proc. 1281.99 In The Amount Of 13,725; Declaration Of Ruben Guerra
Off Calendar May-23-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Atty. RUBEN GUERRA, Esq. (via Zoom videoconference) of Guerra & Casillas LLP (tel: 213-437-9495) for Plaintiff.
Atty. YAKOV WIEGMANN, Esq. of Wilson, Elser, Moskowitz Edelman & Dicker LLP (tel: 415-433-0990) for Defendant Uber Technologies, Inc.

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF FELICITA FELIX SARAVIA V. DE MONTOYA's Motion To Vacate Order Compelling Jams Arbitration And Request For Sanctions Under Cal. Civ. Proc. 1281.99 In The Amount Of 13,725.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Felicita Saravia's motion to vacate order compelling arbitration is granted and her request for monetary sanctions is granted in part. The order compelling arbitration is vacated and the case is restored to the civil active list. Ms. Saravia is awarded sanctions in the amount of $6,900 against defendant Uber Technologies, Inc. to be paid no later than June 20, 2025.

The court, not the arbitrator, decides whether a party has defaulted under CCP 1281.97(a). If there is a default, the plaintiff may "proceed in a court of appropriate jurisdiction.. (CCP 1281.97(b)(1); see also Sanders v. Superior Court (May 6, 2025) 2025 WL 1303386 at *6. Moreover, the court determines the gateway issue whether an arbitration agreement-including a delegation clause-is enforceable between the parties. (Sandquist v. Lebo Automotive, Inc. (2016) 1 Cal.5th 233, 252 ; Rent-A-Center, W., Inc. v. Jackson (2010) 561 U.S. 63, 72.)

The Federal Arbitration Act ("FAA") does not preempt application of CCP 1281.97. It is unclear whether the parties' agreement "expressly designate[s] that any arbitration proceeding should move forward under the FAA's procedural provisions rather than under state procedural law." (Cronus Investments, Inc. v. Concierge Servs. (2005) 55 Cal.4th 376, 394 (emphasis in original).) The agreement does not expressly refer to the FAA's procedural rules. Even if the FAA procedural rules apply, the FAA does not have a timely payment rule and the parties agreed: "If the FAA and applicable arbitration provider's rules are found to not apply to any issue regarding the interpretation or enforcement of this Arbitration Agreement, then that issue shall be resolved under the laws of the state where you reside when you accept these Terms." (Id.) Therefore, the CAA applies. Nor is there conflict or obstacle preemption because CCP 1281.97 furthers the FAA's goal of prompt arbitration. (Sanders, 2025 WL 1303386 at *9.)

The court rejects Uber's request to deny without prejudice or continue this motion pending disposition of Hernandez v. Sohnen Enterprises (2024) 102 Cal.App.5th 222 (review granted, Aug. 21, 2024, Case No. S285696). The court of appeal in Sanders believed that it should issue a decision notwithstanding the pendency of Hernandez before the California Supreme Court and this court believes that it should follow the lead of Sanders, especially since that decision is well-reasoned. If the California Supreme Court reaches a contrary decision, Uber may file a motion for reconsideration or invite the court to reconsider on its own motion

Per CCP 1281.99(a), the plaintiff is entitled to sanctions (including attorney fees) "as a result of the material breach." The court awards plaintiff $975 for the time spent in the arbitration proceedings and $5,925 for the expenses of this motion for a total of $6,900. Plaintiff is not entitled to recovery of expenses incurred in opposing the underlying motion to compel arbitration because those expenses were not incurred as "a result of the material breach."

Order submitted.

Judge: Harold E. Kahn; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Court reporter: Jaime Ply, CSR# 13323 (tel: 714-316-3757 / email: jplycsr@aol.com) =(301/HEK)
